title: Drumapuṣpikā — The First Chapter of the Daśavaikālika Sūtra (Sajjhāy)
summary: This is the opening chapter of the Daśavaikālika, one of Jainism's oldest monastic scriptures, recited here as a sajjhāy (sacred study-recitation). In just five short verses it teaches that true religion (dharma) — built on non-violence, self-restraint, and austerity — is the highest blessing, and then paints a beautiful picture: a good monk should live like a honey-bee, gathering food gently from many households without harming or burdening anyone, and without attachment. It is recited to fix the heart on this ideal of harmless, contented, self-controlled living.
ritualPlacement: In the Devasi (daily, sunset) pratikramaṇa, Drumapuṣpikā is the first item of the closing sajjhāy (svādhyāya) cluster — recited after Muni Vandanā and followed by Pañca-Parameṣṭhi Sajjhāy and Mannaha Jiṇāṇaṁ Sajjhāy. A sajjhāy is study-recitation for the closing study-portion and is NOT one of the six Āvaśyakas. Within this corpus the chapter appears in the Devasi flow; it is not part of the shared core-sūtra set that carries the pakkhī/cāumāsī/saṁvatsarī word-substitution, so that substitution rule does not apply to it.
authorAndSource: Source text: Daśavaikālika-sūtra, chapter 1 (Drumapuṣpikā), a mūla-sūtra of the Śvetāmbara Jain canon composed in Ardhamāgadhī Prākrit. Traditionally attributed to Ācārya Śayyambhava (Sejjambhava), who according to Jain tradition compiled the Daśavaikālika by drawing from the Pūrva literature for the instruction of his disciple-son Manaka. Here it is reproduced as the sajjhāy text printed in the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) edition Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit.
structureArc: Verse 1 states the thesis: dharma — i.e. ahiṁsā, saṁyama, tava — is the supreme auspiciousness, and even the gods bow to one whose mind is ever set on it. Verses 2–5 then unfold a single sustained simile to show what such a dharma-grounded mendicant looks like in daily conduct. Verse 2 gives the image: a bee draws nectar from a tree's blossoms without wearying the flower, yet nourishes itself. Verse 3 applies it: the liberated śramaṇas, the true sādhus of the world, move among households for alms like birds among flowers. Verse 4 voices their own resolve: 'we gain our livelihood and no being is harmed; we range only among food already prepared (for the householder's own use), as bees among flowers.' Verse 5 closes with the defining mark and a play on words: those wise ones, unattached and self-restrained, devoted to varied gathered alms, are 'like honey-bees' (madhukara-samā) — and for this very reason are called sādhus.
recurringImagery: The governing image across verses 2–5 is the BEE AND THE FLOWER (the mādhukarī / 'bee-like' mode of seeking alms, gocarī/gochari). Just as a bee (bhramara/madhukara) sips nectar from many blossoms without breaking, withering, or exhausting any single flower, and moves on satisfied, so the ideal mendicant gathers his food in small amounts from many households (mādhukarī-vṛtti), taking only what is freely available and already prepared for the householders' own needs (ahākaḍa / yathākṛta), causing no harm, no special burden, and no depletion to anyone. The bee touches lightly and leaves the flower unharmed: this encodes the chapter's core ethics — ahiṁsā (non-harm), aparigraha/non-attachment (anissiya = unattached, not relying on any one giver), saṁtoṣa (contentment with little), and saṁyama (restraint). The brief variant image in verse 3 — the mendicant as a free-flying BIRD (vihaṅgama) among flowers — reinforces the same idea of light, unpossessive movement. The wordplay in verse 5 (madhukara-samā / 'honey-maker-like') ties the simile back to the very name sādhu.
keyThemes: Ahiṁsā, saṁyama, and tava as the threefold substance of dharma
Non-harm and non-burden to the giver
Non-attachment and reliance on no single household
Even the gods honor one settled in dharma
What truly makes one a sādhu

glossary.definition: Righteous religion / moral law; here explicitly defined as ahiṁsā, saṁyama, and tava.
Auspiciousness, blessing; dharma is declared the highest (ukkiṭṭha) maṅgala.
Non-violence; refraining from harm to any living being — the first pillar of dharma.
Self-restraint, control of senses and conduct.
A striving Jain ascetic / monk (Prakrit samaṇa).
A holy mendicant; the verse derives the title from the bee-like, harmless mode of life.
Bee; the central simile for the mendicant who gathers gently without harm.
Honey-bee / honey-maker; verse 5 calls the wise 'madhukara-samā', bee-like.
Tree; whose flowers (puṣpa) the bee visits — root of the chapter-name Drumapuṣpikā.
Flower/blossom (Prakrit puppha), the source of the bee's nectar.
Bird, a sky-goer; the free, unpossessive flyer to which the mendicant is also likened (v.3).
Food 'as already prepared' for the householder's own use (Prakrit ahākaḍa), which alone the monk may accept — faultless alms.
The disciplined seeking/inspection of alms (here dāna-bhatta-esaṇā, seeking of alms-food).
Alms-food gathered as a 'lump'; nāṇā-piṇḍa = devoted to varied alms collected from many homes.
Unattached, dependent on no single support or household (Prakrit aṇissiya).
Tamed, self-controlled; one who has mastered the senses.
The 'bee-like' rule of alms-seeking: a little from many homes, harming none — the conduct this chapter enjoins.
Svādhyāya, sacred study-recitation; the genre in which this chapter is recited (not one of the six Āvaśyakas).

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as one vivid lesson — 'be like a bee': live gently, take little, harm no one, cling to nothing — with verse 1 giving the reason (dharma is the greatest blessing) and verses 2–5 giving the picture. An advanced practitioner should hear the precise monastic ethics encoded in the Prakrit terms — ahākaḍa (yathākṛta) alms, aṇissiya non-reliance, the eṣaṇā-samiti — and the deliberate etymological play in verse 5 that grounds the very word sādhu in the bee-like life.

sources.title: Dashavaikalika Sutra (Daśavaikālika), Chapter 1 'Drumapushpika'
sources.consultedFor: Text and meaning of the five verses; bee-and-flower simile; attribution to Ācārya Śayyambhava

sources.title: Daśavaikālika-sūtra — Wisdomlib entry
sources.consultedFor: Authorship by Śayyambhava, Ardhamāgadhī composition, structure of the ten chapters

sources.title: Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sajjhāy text and its placement in the Devasi closing sajjhāy cluster

notes: Attribution to Ācārya Śayyambhava and the tradition that he extracted the Daśavaikālika from the Pūrvas for his disciple-son Manaka is the standard Śvetāmbara account; precise dating is traditional, not firmly historical. Orthography of the verses follows the Anchalgachchha book verbatim (e.g. 'tava', 'puffa', 'ahāgaḍa', 'aṇissiya', 'sāhūṇo'); these Prakrit spellings should not be normalized. Glossary scriptForms pair the book's Gujarati-script Prakrit with the Sanskrit equivalent where helpful. The chapter is recited here as sajjhāy and is not subject to the pakkhī/cāumāsī/saṁvatsarī word-substitution that governs the shared core sūtras.
